answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Sair-e-Falak* ("Celestial Journey," *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 18), the speaker, accompanied by Imagination (*Takhayyul*), ascends through the heavens on a visionary flight.[^1] What the speaker sees begins with cosmic vastness—the stars gazing in wonder, breaking beyond the "ring of dusk and dawn"—then shifts to paradisiacal splendor: Tooba's bough alive with musical birds, unveiled Houris, and the graceful Saqian offering goblets amid cries to "Drink, and drink again!"[^2] The view darkens to a distant "grim abode": an icy void of ultimate cold (deeper than Qais's gloom or Laila's hair, shaming the Sphere of Zamhareer), revealed by the angel Surosh as Hell itself—not a place of inherent fire, but an empty realm where arriving souls "bring their own embers, from the lives they wrought."[^3]

This expansive, ascending cosmic field of view is determined by the speaker's nature as an imaginative traveler (*humsafar*: companionate, dynamic seeker), who actively "flew onward" through sealed secrets and earthly limits, perceiving heavenly layers (prize of Iram, paradise, hell) as revelations tied to inner potential.[^4]

In *The Wanderer* (*Old English Elegies*, ch. 11), by contrast, the speaker—an exiled "lone-dweller" (*anhaga*) and "earth-stepper," heart-weary from lost kin and lord—contemplates a fallen earthly realm from the margins of exile, amid rime-cold seas and winter storms.[^5] What he sees is relentless mutability and decay on "middle-earth": crumbling wine-halls and frost-covered walls (once giant-works, now storm-battered ruins), vanished joys (horse, warrior, treasure-giver, feast-benches), proud thanes felled by war, bird, wolf, or grave; wealth and kin "loaned" and emptied by *wyrd* (fate), with hail, blizzard, and darkness binding the world in hardship.[^6] Glimpses of the sea-birds, fallow waves, and ghostly walls evoke sorrowful memory over direct heavenly access, though wisdom hints at ultimate "stability" from the heavenly Father.[^7]

This ground-level, retrospective vision of transience—earthly glory "grown dark under night's helm"—stems from the speaker's embodied exile and grief-bound wisdom: stripped of homeland, gold-friend, and hall-joys, he "binds fast" his spirit in patient reflection on human finitude, unable to transcend without first enduring "his share of winters."[^8]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 18 (*Sair-e-Falak*), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/18-sair-e-falak/translation/#p-7bcfb8).
[^2]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 18, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/18-sair-e-falak/translation/#p-11ceb3).
[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 18, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/18-sair-e-falak/translation/#p-4f70cd).
[^4]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 18, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/18-sair-e-falak/translation/).
[^5]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e).
[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).
[^8]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
